heb.paragraph: "The LORD is my light (ʾōrî) and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" (v.1). Light (revelation), salvation (rescue), stronghold (refuge) — three identities of God produce fearlessness. David asks one thing: to dwell in God’s house all his days, to gaze on God’s beauty and seek him in his temple (v.4).

refs.note: "I am the light of the world" — Jesus claims to be the light of Psalm 27:1.
"For to me, to live is Christ" — Paul’s "one thing" echoes David’s.

notes.note: "The LORD is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" Three identities, one conclusion: no fear.
"One thing I ask... to gaze on the beauty of the LORD." Singular desire. The ultimate simplification of the spiritual life.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'my light and my salvation' — 'ôr as a divine attribute is rare in the Psalter. Light dispels darkness, ignorance, and danger simultaneously. The double metaphor (light + salvation) eliminates both confusion and peril.

Alter: 'one thing I ask' — the Hebrew 'aḥat is emphatic. In a world of competing desires, the psalmist has distilled everything to a single longing: to dwell in the presence of God. This is the psalm's theological centre.

Calvin: light, salvation, and stronghold — three metaphors for one reality. Calvin: God illumines the mind, rescues from danger, and fortifies against assault. The triple declaration leaves no room for fear.
Calvin: 'to gaze on the beauty of the Lord' — Calvin reads this as contemplative worship, the highest human activity. Not merely attending the temple but beholding God's perfections within it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'my light' — 'ôrî is used of God only here and in Mic 7:8 and Isa 60:1. The metaphor may draw on ancient Near Eastern solar deity imagery, reappropriated for Yahweh as sole source of illumination.

NET: 'in his dwelling' (sukkōh) — sukkāh is a temporary shelter or booth, evoking the wilderness tabernacle and the feast of Sukkot. God's protection is both intimate (a booth) and sacred (his dwelling).

Kidner: the psalm opens with three names for God and two rhetorical questions. Each name answers a different fear: darkness (light), danger (salvation), attack (stronghold). The questions expect the answer: no one.
Kidner: 'one thing I ask' is not escapism but priority. The psalmist does not deny the enemies of v.2 or the army of v.3. He simply knows what matters more than any of them.

VanGemeren: the confidence declaration (vv.1-6) draws on exodus and conquest imagery — God as light recalls the pillar of fire; God as salvation recalls the sea crossing. The psalmist claims Israel's story for personal faith.

VanGemeren: 'to gaze on the beauty of the Lord' — nō'am means pleasantness, delight. The temple experience is not duty but beauty. Worship at its highest is aesthetic — beholding the attractiveness of God's character.

Goldingay: 'whom shall I fear?' — the question is not about the absence of threats but their irrelevance. The threats in vv.2-3 are vivid and real; the confidence is not denial but defiance grounded in theology.
Goldingay: the shelter imagery shifts from booth (sukkāh) to tent ('ōhel) to rock (ṣûr). Each image is more permanent than the last — God's protection escalates from temporary to eternal.

hist.context: David’s confidence: even if an army besieges him, his heart will not fear (v.3). "One thing I ask: to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze on the beauty of the LORD" (v.4). The "one thing" is singular focus: God’s presence above all else.

heb.paragraph: "Wait (qawwēh) for the LORD; be strong and take heart and wait for the LORD" (v.14). The psalm’s final word is spoken twice: wait. Active, confident waiting — not passive resignation but expectant endurance. The one who asked to "gaze on the beauty of the LORD" (v.4) now waits for that beauty to be revealed.

refs.note: "Those who wait on the LORD will renew their strength" — the same waiting-produces-strength theology.
"The LORD is good to those whose hope is in him, to the one who seeks him" — the same confident waiting.

notes.note: "Though my father and mother forsake me, the LORD will receive me." The ultimate safety net: when human relationships fail, divine love holds.
"I remain confident: I will see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living. Wait for the LORD." Confidence + patience. See goodness + wait. The psalm ends with active hope.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'My heart says of you, "Seek his face!"' — the Hebrew is difficult. Alter reads the heart as echoing a divine command, then the psalmist responding with obedience: 'Your face, LORD, I will seek.'

Alter: 'I remain confident' — the Hebrew has dots above the word lûlē' ('unless'), an ancient scribal notation suggesting textual uncertainty. The conditional may be a broken-off sentence: 'Unless I had believed…'

Calvin: 'Though my father and mother forsake me' — Calvin reads this as the extremity of human abandonment. If even parental love fails, God's does not. The more desperate the condition, the more certain the promise.
Calvin: 'Wait for the Lord' — repeated for emphasis. Calvin: waiting is not passivity but the hardest form of faith. It requires trusting God's timing when our own patience has expired.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: the syntax of v.8 is notoriously difficult. Literally: 'To you my heart says, "Seek my face." Your face, Yahweh, I seek.' The NET reads the psalmist quoting God's invitation and then accepting it.

NET: the dots above lûlē' (the extraordinary points, nequdot) are one of only ten such markings in the Masoretic Text. They may indicate scribal uncertainty about whether the word belongs in the text.

Kidner: the most desolate human condition — abandoned by parents — meets the most extravagant divine promise. Kidner notes that 'receive' (yā'asfēnî) means 'gather' — God collects what others discard.

Kidner: the double command to 'wait' brackets the psalm's final counsel. The repetition is pastoral, not rhetorical — the psalmist knows how hard waiting is and says it twice because once is not enough.

VanGemeren: God as surrogate parent is a deeply pastoral image. When human covenant relationships (even family) collapse, the divine covenant holds. This anticipates the NT theme of adoption into God's family.
VanGemeren: 'the land of the living' contrasts with Sheol. The psalmist expects to see God's goodness not only in the afterlife but in this life. Faith has a present-tense dimension.
Goldingay: the psalm's second half shifts dramatically from confidence (vv.1-6) to lament (vv.7-12). Goldingay argues this is not two psalms but one person holding faith and fear in the same breath.
Goldingay: 'be strong and take heart' — ḥăzaq and 'āmēṣ are military terms. Waiting for God requires the same courage as going into battle. Patience is not weakness; it is strength under discipline.

hist.context: The psalm’s second half shifts to petition: hear me, do not hide your face, do not reject me (vv.7–9). "Though my father and mother forsake me, the LORD will receive me" (v.10). Then the declaration: "I remain confident of this: I will see the goodness of the LORD in the land of the living" (v.13). The psalm closes with the double imperative: wait for the LORD.

ppl.text: The "one thing" is singular focus: God’s presence above all else…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMy heart says of you, “Seek his face!” Your face, Lord, I will seek.ESVYou have said, \u201cSeek my face.\u201d My heart says to you, \u201cYour face, LORD, do I seek.\u201dNIVOne thing I ask from the Lord, this only do I seek: that I may dwell in the house of the Lord all the days of my life, to gaze on the beauty of the Lord and to seek him in his temple.ESVOne thing have I asked of the LORD, that will I seek after: that I may dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life, to gaze upon the beauty of the LORD and to inquire in his temple.

rec.text: ""The LORD is my light and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" Three identities, one conclusion: no fear."

rows.label: The Waiting — 27:7–14
rows.text: "Though father and mother forsake me." "I will see goodness." "Wait for the LORD."

hebtext.note: "The LORD is my light (ʾōrî) and my salvation — whom shall I fear?" (v.1). Light (revelation), salvation (rescue), stronghold (refuge) — three identities of God produce fearlessness. David asks one thing: to dwell in God’s house all his days, to gaze on God’s beauty and seek him in his temple (v.4).

"Wait (qawwēh) for the LORD; be strong and take heart and wait for the LORD" (v.14). The psalm’s final word is spoken twice: wait. Active, confident waiting — not passive resignation but expectant endurance. The one who asked to "gaze on the beauty of the LORD" (v.4) now waits for that beauty to be revealed.

thread.text: "I am the light of the world" — Jesus claims to be the light of Psalm 27:1.
"For to me, to live is Christ" — Paul’s "one thing" echoes David’s.
"Those who wait on the LORD will renew their strength" — the same waiting-produces-strength theology.
"The LORD is good to those whose hope is in him, to the one who seeks him" — the same confident waiting.

themes.note: Faith peaks: the one-thing desire and the double "wait." Worship: gazing on God’s beauty. Mercy: received when parents forsake.
